name: Gancanagh
description: The Gancanagh, also known as the Gean-canagh or Gan Ceanach, is a male fairy in Irish folklore, often depicted as a seductive and melancholic figure. He is known for his irresistible charm and ability to enchant mortal women with his captivating music and sorrowful tales. The Gancanagh is said to appear in lonely glens and valleys, playing haunting melodies on his flute, luring women to their doom with promises of love and eternal happiness. He is often described as being born from the union of fairies and mortals, forever trapped between two worlds. While his intentions may not always be malevolent, his love is considered dangerous and ultimately fatal, leading to a life of sorrow and madness for those who succumb to his allure. The Gancanagh represents the darker, more dangerous side of the fairy realm, a reminder that beauty and enchantment can mask a sinister truth. His existence serves as a cautionary tale about the perils of succumbing to fleeting desires and the allure of the unknown.
subtitle: Fatal fairy of love and sorrow
appearance: The Gancanagh is typically described as a handsome young man, often with a pale complexion and dark, sorrowful eyes. He is usually dressed in green or brightly colored clothing, though some accounts depict him as being unclothed. He is often seen carrying or playing a flute, which he uses to create his enchanting music. Some descriptions include long, flowing hair and an ethereal glow surrounding him.
habitat: Lonely glens, valleys, and secluded areas of the Irish countryside; places associated with sorrow and longing.
behavior: The Gancanagh is primarily a seducer, using his charm and music to lure mortal women. He is often depicted as being melancholy and filled with sorrow, which he uses to manipulate his victims. He promises eternal love and happiness, but his love is ultimately destructive, leading to madness and death. He is often solitary, preferring to dwell in lonely places where he can practice his music and wait for his next victim.

keyMyths.title: The Fatal Flute
keyMyths.description: A woman hears the haunting music of the Gancanagh's flute and is irresistibly drawn to him, forsaking her family and responsibilities. She eventually dies of heartbreak and madness, a victim of his fatal love.
keyMyths.source: Briggs, Katharine Mary. An Encyclopedia of Fairies.

sources.author: W.B. Yeats
sources.work: Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry
sources.description: A collection of Irish folklore, including mentions of the Gancanagh.
sources.text: A collection of Irish folklore, including mentions of the Gancanagh.

sources.author: Patricia Lysaght
sources.description: Explores the broader context of Irish folklore and the fairy realm.
